1 12 0* 0 16 0* 0 11 2*
0 19 2 0 9 7 0 7 8
0 16 0* 0 8 0* 0 6 4*

* Temporary reduction of 16½ per cent.

Note.—The Europe rates of subscription are payable after retirement and during furlough out of India. But an officer on furlough in India and not in receipt of full Indian pay will be required to pay only half the Indian rate of contribution.

Officers deputed to Europe under Article 102 and 103 of the Civil Service Regulations are required to pay the Indian rate of contribution.

TABLE VII.—Additional Monthly Contribution, payable by all Classes alike for each Child living on the first day of the month.
— Fixed Sterling Rates. Reduced temporarily 16½ per cent.
s. d. s. d.
For a son, until age of 21 ... .. 1 11 1 7
For a daughter, until marriage ... .. 4 10 4 0

Note.—The contributions and donations to be paid by Native Members of the Covenanted Medical Service are fixed for the present at an increase of 5 per cent. on the rates paid by Europeans.

Arrears.

13. Interest at the rate of 4½ per cent. per annum will be charged on arrears. Donations will become due from the date on which the liability is incurred, viz., on marriage, on promotion,† or on the birth of a child; but interest will not be charged on sums paid within one month from the date on which they become due.

Reports of Marriages, Births, and Deaths.

14. An officer who is married at the date of his admission into the service must report the fact of his marriage at the time, and the names and dates of birth of his children, if any have been born to him. A member in the service must report his marriage, or the birth of a child

born in wedlock, or the death of a wife or child, within one month of the occurrence to the Comptroller of Military Accounts in the Presidency.† Forms for this purpose are given in the Appendix.

15. The birth of every child born alive must be reported; but if a child die before it is 30 days old, no payment in respect to it will be required.

15A. If the report of a marriage, or of the birth of a child, be not made within one month of the occurrence, the donation payable in each case will be increased 2½ per cent., and interest thereon charged from the date of marriage, or birth of the child. After six months' delay, donations will be increased 5 per cent., and interest charged as above.

15B. In the event of the report of a marriage, or the birth of a child, not being made by a subscriber during his lifetime, in accordance with the rules, the widow or child will not be entitled to any benefits.

Posthumous Child.

16. A pension will be granted to a posthumous child on payment of the donation prescribed in Table V, which may be recovered by twelve monthly instalments in India or four quarterly instalments in England. The report of the birth of a posthumous child must be made in Form D, the last sentence being omitted.

Commutation of Contributions.

17. Officers may at the date of retirement, or

† Except when promotion is uncertain. In such cases the date on which the liability is incurred will be the date of the "Gazette of India" in which the promotion is notified, or, when a continuous service Royal Artillery or Royal Engineer officer is in England, the date of the "London Gazette."

‡ Or the Director of Funds, India Office, when an officer is in Europe.

at any subsequent period, commute their monthly contribution by a single payment representing the value thereof during the joint lives of husband and wife, and father and child.

In the event of the wife dying after the date of commutation, contribution at the unmarried rate must be resumed, or, as an alternative, it may be commuted.

A subscriber who has commuted will on re-marrying become liable for marriage donation and the difference between the married and unmarried rates of contribution, or the commuted value on the joint lives.

18. Widowers and bachelors may in like manner commute their monthly contribution for life. On each subsequent marriage they will become liable for the donation and difference of contribution laid down in the previous article.

Donation and contribution or its commuted value must be paid for all children born to a subscriber after his retirement from the service.

Duration of Pensions.

19. If a widow re-marry her pension will cease during coverture, but if she again become a widow she will be entitled to revert to her pension as widow of her first husband, or, if her last husband have been a contributor, to receive a pension at her option as the widow of her last husband. A widow can only receive one pension.

20. The pension of a male orphan is payable until he attains the age of 21 years.

20A. Subscribers can make special provision for pension to sons who may become permanently incapacitated from earning a livelihood.

The pensions so provided for will commence at the age of 21, if the son be then fatherless, or, after that age, on his becoming fatherless, and as regards the amounts of such pensions, and the subscriptions to be paid to secure them, each case will be considered and determined on its merits.

21. The mother or guardian of a male orphan who has completed the age of 16 may, at any time thereafter (on application in Forms F to H annexed to these regulations), have the residue of his pension commuted into a single payment, for the due application of which the mother or guardian will be held responsible. The following table gives the commuted value of such pensions:—

TABLE VIII.—Showing the present Value of the Pension of a Male Orphan according to his Age.

Years. 16 17 18 19 20
Months. £ £ £ £ £
0 129 106 81 56 28
3 123 100 75 49 21
6 117 94 68 42 14
9 111 87 62 35 7

22. The pension of a female orphan continues until her marriage, and then ceases absolutely. It does not revive on widowhood.

Good Conduct.

23. The pensions provided under these regulations shall, in all cases, be held during good behaviour only, and will be withheld if any pensioner is found to be leading an immoral life, or on proof of any gross misconduct. The order of the Secretary of State for India in Council in any such case shall be final and conclusive.

Alienation of Pension.

24. A pension paid under these regulations may not be transferred or assigned, or in any way alienated, from the pensioner to whom or for whose benefit it is payable.

Passage Money.

25. An officer may, by a special contribution, provide passage money from India for his widow and orphans. The amount so secured will belong to the officer's estate, but will be primarily applicable to the provision of passage money and outfit for any member or members of his family (wife and children) who may be in India at the time of his death. If the whole amount is not required for the above purpose, the balance will be paid over to the officer's estate. The amount secured must not exceed 1,500 rupees, and a health certificate must in every case be furnished by the subscriber. The value will be determined by the tables in Major-General Hannyngton's report.

25A. The special contribution can be paid by monthly instalments not exceeding 24, the instalments in arrears bearing interest at 4\frac{1}{2} per cent. per annum, and in the event of a subscriber dying before completion of the payment, the balance due at the date of his death will be deducted from the sum insured.

25B. Native commissioned medical officers are required to pay, as in the case of their ordinary contributions, 5 per cent. over and above the ordinary rates of special contribution.

25C. In the event of a subscriber's family not having occasion to make use of the passage money for which special contribution has been made, it will be paid to his estate on his death, irrespective of whether this occurs before or after his retirement, or whether he continued to subscribe to the pension branch up to the date of his death.

If an officer on retirement desires to sever his connection with the insurance branch, he will be allowed the option of receiving the value of the sum assured at the date of withdrawal from the pension branch.

If, however, the option be then declined, the sum originally assured will be paid in accordance with the rules at the decease of the subscriber.

Transfer to Her Majesty's Corps and Officers who resign.

26. Officers who may be transferred from the Indian Staff Corps to Her Majesty's Line regiments, and officers who resign the service, will be entitled to receive back such portion of their contributions as may be in excess of the risk borne during their membership, but they cannot continue subscribers.

Half-pay.

27. A married officer on half-pay must continue to subscribe at Indian rates* for full benefits, or at half the rates appertaining to his class for half benefits in respect to his wife's contingent pension, but full rates for children must be continued for full benefits. In the event of his return to full pay he will revert to full rates and benefits. Unmarried officers must continue to subscribe at the Europe rate of subscription.

Retired Officers.

28. Married officers who retire from the service on a pension may continue to subscribe at the Europe rate of subscription, or they may withdraw, receiving back such portion of their contributions as is in excess of the risk borne during the term of their membership; but the usual subscription will be deducted from the pension of all such officers until they shall have intimated in writing their intention of withdrawing. Un-

married officers may continue their subscription on the same terms as married officers, but will not be entitled on retirement to any refund in the event of their withdrawal.

Dismissed Officers.

29. An officer who is dismissed the service by sentence of a court-martial, or by other competent authority, will cease, ipso facto, to be a contributor under these regulations, and will have no claim to any refund. His family will not be entitled to any pensions under these regulations.

Certificates.

30. Declarations as to continued widowhood and the survival of children must be furnished to the Accountant-General, India Office, by whom pensions in Europe are paid under the authority of the Director of Funds.

APPENDIX.

Note.—In England all reports and applications for pensions must be made to the Director of Funds, India Office, Whitehall, London, S.W.

FORM (A) of a Letter from an Officer with whom it is optional under Regulation 2 to contribute under these regulations, announcing his intention to do so.

To the Controller of Military Accounts for the Presidency of

Sir,—I have the honour to announce my intention of contributing for pensions for my family under the regulations published with the Notification by the Government of India, in the Military Department, No. 1315, dated 28th December, 1872.

I agree to be bound in every respect by the said regulations, and by any orders in modification thereof that may be hereafter passed by the Secretary of State for India in Council.

I was born on the

My wife was born on the

We were married on the 18

A certified copy of the register of our marriage is herewith transmitted.

The names and birthdays of my children are as follows:—

the service of the Government of India, married, on the 18

I was born on the

My wife was born on the

The names and birthdays of my children are as follows:—

I was born on the

Sir,—I have the honour to report the birth to me of a male (female) child on the

be entered in the pension register kept under the regulations published with the Notification by the Government of India in the Military Department, No. 1315, dated 28th December, 1872, and that the deductions thereby prescribed may be made from my pay.

(See Regulation 14.)

To the Controller of Military Accounts for the Presidency of

Sir,—I have the honour to report the decease on the of my son (daughter) born on the (or of my wife)

FORM (F) of Application for the Commuted Value of the Pension of a Male Orphan over 16 years of age. (See Regulation 21.)

Sir,—I, A. B., being the mother (or guardian) of C. D., son of E. F., late a in the service of the Government of India, having now an opportunity of placing my son (or ward) in a suitable position, request that the commuted value of the remaining portion of his pension may be granted to me under my guarantee for its proper application to his use.

The age of my son (or ward) is now years

months, and I submit herewith a certificate of his health.

FORM (G).—Medical Certificate to be furnished by Mother or Guardian applying for Commutation of a Male Orphan's Annuity. (See Regulation 21.)

We do hereby certify that we have carefully and personally examined into the state of the health of C. D., son of the late E. F., and that we consider him free from any disease likely to prevent his attaining the age of 21 years.

(Sd.) G. H. } M.D.
" I. K. }

FORM (H).—Mother's or Guardian's Receipt for Commuted Value of a Male Orphan's Annuity. (See Regulation 21.)

I, A. B., mother (or guardian) of C. D., son of the late E. F., in consideration of the payment to me of the sum of being the commuted value of the pension receivable by the said C. D., from till he attains the age of 21 years, do hereby bind myself to account, if called on by the Secretary of State for India, for the due application of the said sum for the use and benefit of the said C. D.

MILITARY (LATE LORD CLIVE'S) FUND.WIDOWS' PENSIONS.

1. The widows of officers who were originally in the old Indian service are alone eligible for this pension.

2. They will be required to make a statutory declaration as to the amount of real and personal property possessed by their late husbands and by themselves; and no widow will be eligible for the full pension if the total of such property amounts to the following sums:—

3. The rates of pensions are as follows:—

In the case of the widow of a colonel ... £4,000
" " " a lieutenant-
" ant-colonel ... " " " " " 3,000
In the case of the widow of a major ... 2,500
" " " a captain ... 2,000
" " " a lieutenant 1,000

Life interests and annuities are capitalised for the purpose of calculating these limits under rules laid down by the Secretary of State in Council.

Ranks. Widows' Pensions.
Per diem. Per annum.
s. d. £ s. d.
Colonel, Surgeon-Major-General, and Surgeon-Colonel ... 6 3 114 1 3
Lieut.-Colonel, Brigade-Surgeon-Lieut.-Colonel, and Surgeon-Lieut.-Colonel... .. 5 0 91 5 0
Major, Surgeon-Major, and Chaplain of 15 years' service ... 3 9 68 8 9
Captain, Surgeon, Chaplain of 7 years' service, and Veterinary Surgeon of 20 years' service ... .. 2 6 45 12 6
Chaplain under 7 years' service ... .. 1 9 31 18 9
Lieut. and Veterinary Surgeon ... .. 1 3 22 16 3

Pensions at diminished rates (the amount of which will be decided by the Secretary of State in Council) will, however, be granted in certain cases in which the property possessed exceeds the above limits. A diminished pension cannot be granted of a lower amount than £10. This modification of the old rule has effect from 18th February, 1890.

4. The pension of a widow may be withheld or discontinued should her conduct be such as to

render her, in the opinion of the Secretary of State, unworthy of it.

5. The pension is liable to be discontinued or reduced if at any time after the officer's death his widow receives any accession of property or income.

6. Pensions lapse on re-marriage and are not restored on a second widowhood.

7. Pensioners neglecting to claim the pension for three half-years will be considered as dead;

and no arrears for a longer period than two years back from the date of application for admission or re-admission, as the case may be, will be allowed either to claimants or to pensioners after admission.

8. The widows of officers formerly of the Indian service, who have joined the British army, are permitted to make their choice between these pensions and Royal Warrant pensions.

9. The widows of officers who have retired from the service are not eligible for the benefit of these pensions.*

10. The widows of officers who at the time of their death were pensioners on the Military (late Lord Clive's) Fund, are entitled to the benefits of the Fund provided they were married to their husbands prior to the latter becoming pensioners on the Fund.

11. The pensions are payable quarterly, on the 1st January, 1st April, 1st July, and 1st October.

12. In the event of any question arising concerning the qualifications of widows claiming these pensions, the decision rests with the Secretary of State in Council.

13. These regulations are subject to any alterations which may be decided on by the Secretary of State in Council.

INDIA OFFICE,
January, 1895.

Note.—Applications for pensions should be addressed to—

The Military Secretary,
India Office,
London, S.W.

REGULATIONS FOR THE EXAMINATION OF CANDIDATES FOR ADMISSION TO HER MAJESTY'S INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

1. The regulations are those in force at the present time. They are subject to any alterations that may be determined on.

2. Candidates must be natural-born subjects of Her Majesty between 21 and 28 years of age at the date of the Examination, of sound bodily health, and in the opinion of the Secretary of State for India in Council in all respects suitable to hold commissions in the Indian

Medical Service. They may be married or unmarried. They must possess a diploma or diplomas entitling them under the Medical Acts to practise both medicine and surgery in Great Britain and Ireland.

3. They must subscribe and send in to the Military Secretary, India Office, Westminster, so as to reach that address at least a fortnight before the date fixed for the Examination, a declaration according to the annexed form.

Declaration to be submitted.

4. This declaration must be accompanied by the following documents:—
a. Proof of age, either by Registrar General's certificate, or where such certificate is unattainable, by the candidate's own statutory declaration, form of which can be obtained at the India Office, supported, if required by the Secretary of State, by such evidence as he may consider satisfactory. A certificate of baptism which does not afford proof of age will be useless.

b. A recommendation from some person of standing in society—not a member of his own family—to the effect that he is of regular and steady habits and likely in every respect to prove creditable to the service if admitted; and a certificate of moral character from a magistrate, or a minister of the religious denomination to which the candidate belongs.

c. A certificate of registration, under the Medical Acts, of the degrees, diplomas, and licences possessed by the candidate.

5. The physical fitness of each candidate will be determined previous to examination, by a Board of Medical Officers, who are required to certify that his vision is sufficiently good to enable him to perform any surgical operation without the aid of glasses. A moderate

Physical Examination.

degree of myopia† will not be considered a disqualification, provided it does not necessitate the use of glasses during the performance of operations, and that no organic disease of the eyes exists.

Every candidate must also be free from all other organic disease, and from constitutional weakness, or other disability likely to unfit him for military service in India.

6. On producing the foregoing qualifications,† the candidate will be examined by the Examining Board in the following compulsory subjects, and the highest number of marks attainable will be distributed as follows:—

Subjects for Examination.

* Except in the case of the widows of General Officers compulsorily retired under the provisions of the Royal Warrants of 31st December, 1877, and 1st May, 1878.

† Further particulars as to the standard of eyesight will be found in the pamphlet by Sir J. Fayrer, M.D., K.C.S.I., &c., entitled "Regulations as to Defects of Vision," published by Messrs. J. and A. Churchill, 11, New Burlington Street, London, W.

‡ Candidates who pass the Medical Board Examination will be required to pay a fee of £1 before being permitted to compete.

Marks.
a. Anatomy and Physiology ... .. 1,000
b. Surgery ... .. 1,000
c. Medicine, including Therapeutics, the diseases of women and children ... .. 1,000
d. Chemistry and Pharmacy, and a practical knowledge of Drugs ... .. 1,000

N.B.—The Examination in Medicine and Surgery will be in part practical, and will include operations on the dead body, the application of surgical apparatus, and the examination of medical and surgical patients at the bedside. The Examination in Chemistry will be limited to the elements of the Science, and to its application to Medicine, Pharmacy and Practical Hygiene.

No candidate shall be considered eligible who shall not have obtained at least one third of the marks obtainable in each of the above compulsory subjects and one half of the aggregate marks for all the compulsory subjects.

7. Candidates may be examined in the following voluntary subjects, for which the maximum number of marks obtainable will be:—

Voluntary subjects. Marks.
French, German, and Hindustani (150 each) ... .. 450
Natural Sciences ... .. 300

The Natural Sciences will include comparative Anatomy, Zoology, Natural Philosophy, Physical Geography, and Botany with special reference to Materia Medica.

A number less than one third of the marks obtainable in any of these voluntary subjects will not be allowed to count in favour of a candidate who has qualified in the compulsory subjects.

The knowledge of Modern Languages being considered of great importance, all intending competitors are urged to qualify in French and German.

Candidates presenting themselves for Examination in Hindustani are recommended to study some recognised text-book, such as the annotated edition of the 'Taubat-un-Nasuk,' which is the text used by candidates for the Indian Civil Service.

8. The appointments announced for competition will be filled up from the list of qualified candidates arranged in order of merit as finally determined by the total number of marks each has obtained in both the compulsory and voluntary subjects.

The Examiners in London will prepare a list in order of merit, with the marks affixed in the different subjects, to be transmitted to the Director-General, and communicated to the Professors of the Army Medical School. If any candidate is found to be deficient in any particular subject, this shall be stated, in order that he may receive special instruction on the point at Netley.

9. After passing this Examination, the successful candidates will be required to attend one entire course of practical instruction at the Army Medical School in—

  1. (1.) Hygiene.
  2. (2.) Clinical and Military Medicine.
  3. (3.) Clinical and Military Surgery.
  4. (4.) Pathology of diseases and injuries incidental to Military Service.

These courses are to be of not less than four months' duration; but candidates who have already gone through a course at Netley as candidates for the Army Medical Service may, if thought desirable, be exempted from attending the School a second time.

10. During the period of residence at the Army Medical School each candidate will receive an allowance of 8s. per diem, with quarters, or when quarters are not provided, with the usual fuel and light allowances of a subaltern, to cover all costs of maintenance; and he will be required to provide himself with uniform (viz., the regulation undress uniform of a Surgeon-Lieutenant of the British Service, but without the sword).

11. All candidates will be required to conform to such rules of discipline as the Senate may from time to time enact.

12. At the conclusion of the course, candidates will be required to pass an Examination on the subjects taught in the School. The Examination will be conducted by the Professors of the School.

The Director-General, or any Medical Officer deputed by him, may be present and take part in the Examination. If the candidate gives satisfactory evidence of being qualified for the practical duties of an Army Medical Officer he will be eligible for a commission as Surgeon-Lieutenant.

13. The position of the candidates on the list of Surgeon-Lieutenants will be determined by the combined results of the preliminary and of the final Examinations, and, so far as the requirements of the Service will permit, they will have the choice of Presidency in India, according to their position in that list.

14. No candidate will be permitted to compete more than twice.

India Office,
November, 1894.

Examinations for admission to the Service usually take place twice in the year, i.e., in February and August.

N.B.—There will be an Examination for not less than twelve appointments in August, 1896. The exact date will be notified in the newspapers when fixed. The number of appointments to be competed for at subsequent Examinations cannot at present be stated.

Candidates cannot be supplied with copies of past Examination papers.

DECLARATION AND SCHEDULE OF QUALIFICATIONS.

(To be filled up by Candidates.)

Christian and Sur-name at full length.

I _____
_____ Years of Age in _____ last, vide accom-panying Certificate, a Candidate for employment in Her Majesty's Indian Medical Service, do hereby attest my readiness to engage for that Service, and to proceed on duty immediately on being gazetted.

I declare that I labour under no Mental or Constitutional Disease, nor any imperfection or disability that can interfere with the most efficient discharge of the Duties of a Medical Officer.

The Dates of Graduations and the University or Colleges are to be stated.

I have the Degree of A.M. or A.B. from the _____
I have the Degree of M.D. or M.B. from the _____
I have a Licence to Practise Medicine from the _____
I have a Diploma in Surgery from the _____
I have a Licence to Practise Surgery from the _____

The Candidate should here state whether he has served in the Medical Department of the Army or Navy or in the Sub-ordinate Medical Department in India.

(Signature at full length) _____

(Date) _____

(Place of Residence) _____

Candidates who desire to undergo the voluntary examination are to sign the following declaration :—

It is my intention to undergo the voluntary examination in*

*State what subjects will be taken up.

N.B.—This paper, when filled up, is to be returned under cover to

The Military Secretary,

India Office,

London,

S.W.

Note.—Candidates competing after the year 1896, will be required to produce, in addition to the certificates specified in paragraph 4 of these regulations, a certificate of having attended a course of instruction during not less than three months at an ophthalmic hospital, or the ophthalmic department of a general hospital, which course shall include instruction in the errors of refraction.

MEMORANDUM REGARDING THE POSITION OF OFFICERS TO BE APPOINTED TO HER MAJESTY'S
INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

India Office, September, 1895.

1. This memorandum is based on the regulations in force at the present time. They are subject to any alteration that may be determined on.

PASSAGE TO INDIA.

2. Officers on appointment are, when possible, provided with passage to India by troop ship; when troop ship accommodation is not available, passage at the public expense is provided by steamer, or a passage allowance granted, if preferred. A charge for messing during the voyage is made at the rate of 2s. a day. This payment does not include the cost of liquors, which are charged for as extras.

3. Any officer who may neglect or refuse to proceed to India under the orders of the Secretary of State for India within two months from the date of leaving Netley, will be considered as having forfeited his appointment, unless special circumstances shall justify a departure from this regulation.

PAY PREVIOUS TO ARRIVAL IN INDIA.

4. The rate of pay drawn by Surgeon-Lieutenants from the date of passing the final examination at the Army Medical School until arrival in India is 10s. a day. (For rates subsequent to their landing in India, see paras. 17, 18, and 19.)

Pay at the above rate is issued in this country up to the date of embarkation, and an advance of two months' pay at the same rate is also made prior to embarkation, which is adjusted in India in accordance with the rate laid down in para. 17.

GRADES AND PRECEDENCE.

Royal Warrant, 7th Dec., 1891. 5. The grades of Officers in the Indian Medical Service are seven in number, viz. :—

1. Surgeon-Major-General. 5. Surgeon-Major.
2. Surgeon-Colonel. 6. Surgeon-Captain.
3. Brigade-Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel. 7. Surgeon-Lieutenant.
4. Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel.

6. These ranks carry precedence and generally other advantages attaching to the rank indicated by the military portion of the title.

PROMOTION.

7. A Surgeon-Lieutenant's Commission dates from the day of passing out of the Army Medical School.

8. A Surgeon-Lieutenant is promoted to Surgeon-Captain on completion of three years' full-pay* service from date of first Commission.

9. A Surgeon-Captain is promoted to Surgeon-Major on completion of 12 years' full-pay* service.
Royal Warrant, 7th Dec., 1891,
Arts. 3-6.

10. A Surgeon-Major is promoted to Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel on completion of 20 years' full-pay* service.

11. All promotions from the rank of Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel to that of Brigade-Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel, from the rank of Brigade-Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel to that of Surgeon-Colonel, and from the rank of Surgeon-Colonel to that of Surgeon-Major-General, are given by selection for ability and merit.

12. On appointment as Honorary Physician or Honorary Surgeon to Her Majesty an Officer below the rank of Surgeon-Colonel is promoted to that rank, remaining supernumerary until absorbed.
Royal Warrant, 7th Dec., 1891,
Art. 8.

13. For distinguished service in the field an Officer of the Indian Medical Service may be promoted from any rank to that next above it, remaining supernumerary in the higher rank until he would have been promoted in ordinary course, or until selected for further promotion for which he is eligible according to his standing in the higher rank.
Royal Warrant, 7th Dec., 1891,
Art. 9.

TENURE OF OFFICE IN ADMINISTRATIVE GRADES.

14. The tenure of office of Surgeon-Major-Generals and Surgeon-Colonels is limited to five years.

Despatches to the Government of India, Nos. 340, dated 7th November, 1864, para. 31, and 150, dated 15th June, 1876.

* See, however, para. 40.

15. Surgeon-Colonels, if not disqualified by age, are eligible either for employment for a second tour of duty in the same grade, or for employment in the higher grade of Surgeon-Major-General by promotion thereto.

16. Absence on leave in excess of eight months during a five years' tour of duty involves forfeiture of appointment.

PAY AND ALLOWANCES.

17. The following are the rates of "Indian pay and allowances" drawn by officers of the Indian Medical Service:—

Despatch to the Government of India, No. 340, dated 7th November, 1864, para. 29, and India Army Circulars, 6th February and 20th May, 1885.

Rank. Years' Service. Indian Pay and Allowances per mensem.
Brigade-Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel and Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel ...
Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel ...
Surgeon-Major ...
" ... 		25 and over When not holding an appointment carrying higher pay.
R. A. P.
888 12 0
852 3 7
677 6 11
640 14 6
Surgeon-Captain ...
" ...
" ...
Surgeon-Captain and Surgeon-Lieutenant ... 		10 "
6 "
5 "
under 5 		A.
When not receiving any Staff Allowance.
R. A. P.
451 14 5
433 10 2
335 12 2
317 8 0
Surgeon-Captain and Surgeon-Lieutenant ... B.
When receiving Staff Allowance in addition. (See para. 19 and note.)
R. A. P.
410 9 5
392 5 2
304 14 2
286 10 0

18. On first appointment they only come into receipt of "Indian pay and allowances" (Column A) from the date of their arrival in India.

Despatch to Government of India, No. 265, of 26th Sept., 1878.

19. Officers holding the principal administrative appointments and substantive military charges of the Indian Medical Service receive at present the following consolidated salaries:—

Despatches to the Government of India, No. 340 of 1864, paras. 28 and 30, and No. 167 of 1866, para. 12; Order of the Government of India in the Home Department, No. 150, dated 15th March, 1880, and Despatch to Government of India, No. 29 of 1895.

Rs. per mensem.
Surgeon-Major-General ... 1 @ ... 2,700
2 @ ... 2,500
2 @ ... 2,200
Surgeon-Colonel ... from ... 1,800
to ... 2,250
Brigade-Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel or Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel of 20 years' service and upwards in substantive charge* of a Native regiment ... 1,000 with Rs. 90, horse allowance in Cavalry regiments.

* Medical charges are of two kinds—substantive and officiating. The first is held by an officer as his fixed and permanent appointment. For this he is entitled to the monthly consolidated pay laid down in the above scale (para. 19), subject to the condition specified in para. 23.

An officiating charge is held by an officer who is acting temporarily in an appointment during the absence of the Officer whose substantive charge it is. The officer thus officiating receives not the consolidated pay of the appointment laid down in the scale (para. 19), but the Indian pay and allowances (para. 17) of his own grade plus half the staff pay of the appointment in which he is officiating (plus half the staff pay of his own appointment, if he have one).

The staff pay of an appointment is the difference between the Indian pay and allowances and the consolidated pay of the permanent incumbent. It has been ruled, however, that the extra allowance for an officiating charge shall never be less than Rs. 100 per mensem.

Example 1. A Surgeon-Captain or Surgeon-Lieutenant of less than 5 years' service officiates for one of the same period of service in charge of a regiment. He receives Rs. 286. 10 + Rs. 100 = Rs. 386. 10.

Example 2. A Surgeon-Captain or Surgeon-Lieutenant of less than 5 years' service, officiating for a Surgeon-Captain of 6 years' service, in charge of a regiment, receives Rs. 286. 10 + \frac{Rs. 600 - Rs. 392. 5. 2}{2} = Rs. 390. 7. 5.

Example 3. A Surgeon-Captain of 5 years' service holding permanent medical charge of a regiment, and officiating for a Surgeon-Major under 20 years' service absent from his regiment, receives Rs. 304. 14. 2 + \frac{800 - 677. 6. 11}{2} = Rs. 513. 11. 7.

Surgeon-Major under 20 years' service in substantive charge of ditto ... .. Rs. per mensem. 800 with Rs. 90, horse allowance in Cavalry regiments.

Surgeon-Captain above 5 years' full-pay service in substantive charge of a Native regiment ... .. 600 with Rs. 60 ditto.

Surgeon-Captain or Surgeon-Lieutenant under 5 years' ditto in substantive charge of a Native regiment ... .. 450 with Rs. 60 ditto.

20. The salaries of other substantive medical appointments in the Civil and Military Departments are consolidated, and vary from Rs. 1,800 to Rs. 400 per mensem.

21. Qualified Officers of the Medical Service are also eligible for appointments in the Assay Department. The salaries of these appointments are from Rs. 600 to Rs. 2,250 per mensem.

Financial Despatch to the Government of India, No. 29, dated 27th January, 1876.

22. Officers are required to perform two years' regimental duty in India before they can be considered eligible for civil employment.

Despatch to the Government of India, No. 235, dated 16th November, 1866, para. 7.

23. No officer, however employed, can receive any staff allowance in addition to the pay laid down in para. 17 unless he has passed the examination in Hindustani known as the "Lower Standard." The passing of this examination does not of itself bring any increase of pay to an officer, unless appointed to a substantive or officiating charge; but failure to pass disqualifies an officer, even when holding such substantive or officiating charge, from receiving any portion of the staff allowances of the appointment.

24. Surgeon-Major-Generals and Surgeon-Colonels, on vacating office at the expiration of the five years' tour of duty, are permitted to draw in India respectively an unemployed salary of Rs. 1,200 per mensem in the former, and Rs. 900 in the latter case, for a period of six months from the date of their vacating office, after which they are placed while unemployed on the following scale of pay:—

Surgeon-Major-General. Surgeon-Colonel.
Per diem. Per diem.
£ s. d. £ s. d.
After 30 years' service on full pay ... .. 2 5 0 1 14 0
" 25 " " ... .. 2 5 0 1 10 0
" 20 " " , or on promotion,
should this period of service not be completed ...		 2 0 0 1 8 0
LEAVE RULES.

(Paras. 25 (2) to 29 apply only to officers in Military employ.)

25. Officers of the Indian Medical Service, below the rank of Surgeon-Colonel may be granted:—

Despatch to India, No. 92, dated 15th April, 1886.

  1. (1.) Privilege leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.
  2. (2.) Leave out of India, for no longer period than one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty, on the following pay:—
£
After arrival in India, on first appointment ... .. 200 a year.
After the commencement of the 5th year's service for pension ... .. 250 "
Ditto, ditto, 10th ditto ... .. 300 "
Ditto, ditto, 15th ditto ... .. 400 "
Ditto, ditto, 20th ditto ... .. 450 "
Ditto, ditto, 25th ditto ... .. 500 "

(3.) Leave in India, but for the period of one year only, on full military pay and half the Staff salary appointment.

26. No extension of leave involving absence from duty for more than two years, whether taken in or out of India, can be granted except on specially urgent grounds and without pay.

27. An officer unable on account of the state of his health to return to duty within the maximum period of two years' absence, unless he is under para. 26 specially granted an extension of leave without pay, is placed on temporary half-pay or the retired list, as the circumstances of the case may require. An officer is also liable to be placed on half-pay or the retired list should his health require an undue amount of leave, whether in or out of India.

28. Leave may be granted at any time, but solely at the discretion of the Commanders-in-Chief, or of the Local Governments in India in the case of officers not under the direct orders of a Commander-in-Chief.

29. An officer on leave, whether in India or out of India, is required to rejoin at once on being recalled to duty, unless certified by a Medical Board as unfit to do so.

30. Officers of the Administrative Grades may be granted one period of leave not exceeding eight months during their tenure of appointment.

HONOURS AND REWARDS.

31. Officers of the Indian Medical Service are eligible for the military distinction of the Order of the Bath, and for good service pensions. Statutes of the Order of the Bath and Despatch to the Government of India, No. 24, of 25th Jan., 1866.

Six of the most meritorious officers are named Honorary Physicians, and six are named Royal Warrant, 30th April, 1887, Art. 12. Honorary Surgeons to Her Majesty.

RETIRING PENSIONS AND HALF-PAY.

32. Officers of the Indian Medical Service are allowed to retire on the following scale of pension, on completion of the required periods of service:—

Despatch to Government of India, No. 112, dated 14th April, 1881.

Per annum.
£
After 30 years' service for pension ... ... ... ... ... ... 700
" 25 " " " " ... ... 500
" 20 " " " " ... ... 365
" 17 " " " " ... ... 292

33. Time of service for pension reckons from date of first commission, and includes all leave taken under the rules quoted in paras. 25 to 30. (See also para 40.)

34. A Surgeon-Major-General, after five years' active employment in India in that appointment, is entitled to retire upon a pension of £350 per annum, in addition to that to which he may be entitled under the above scale. Despatch to Government of India, No. 340, dated 7th November, 1864, paras. 36 to 38.

35. A Surgeon-Colonel is entitled, after five years' active employment in India in that appointment, to retire upon a pension of £250 per annum, in addition to the pension to which he may be entitled under the above scale.

36. In each of the above cases stated in paras. 34 and 35 eight months' absence on leave is allowed to count towards actual service in those grades. (See para. 30.)

37. A Surgeon-Major-General or Surgeon-Colonel who has completed his term of service and has reverted to British pay may reside in Europe, at the same time qualifying for higher pension. Despatch to the Government of India, No. 166, dated 24th June, 1869, para. 17.

38. With a view to maintain the efficiency of the service, all officers of the rank of Brigade-Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel, Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel, and Surgeon-Major are placed on the Retired List when they have attained the age of 55 years, and all Surgeon-Major-Generals and Surgeon-Colonels when they have attained the age of 60 years. In any special case, where it would appear to be for the good of the service that the officer should continue in employment, he may be so continued, subject in each case to the sanction of the Secretary of State for India in Council. Despatch to Government of India, No. 112, dated 14th April, 1881.

39. Officers not entitled to pension, when placed on temporary or permanent half-pay under para. 27 are granted the British rate of half-pay of their military rank, as under:—

Rank. Rates of Half-Pay.
Per Diem. Per Annum.
Brigade-Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel ... s. d. £ s. d.
Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel ... 11 0 200 15 0
Surgeon-Major ... 9 6 173 7 6
Surgeon-Captain ... 7 0 127 15 0
Surgeon-Lieutenant ... 3 0 54 15 0

Officers cannot retire in India on half pay (No. 45, 28th February, 1865).

40. Time (not exceeding one year) passed on temporary half-pay reckons as service for

Despatch to Government of India, No. 130, dated 13th October, 1892. promotion and pension, in the case of an officer placed on half-pay on account of ill-health contracted in the performance of military duty.

41. Officers of the Indian Medical Service are liable, after retirement on pension before completing 30 years' service, to recall to military duty in case of any great emergency arising, up to 55 years of age.

Despatch to Government of India, No. 165, para. 1, dated 11th September, 1890.

WOUND PENSIONS.

42. Officers are entitled to the same allowances on account of wounds and injuries received in action as are granted to combatant officers of Her Majesty's Indian Military Forces holding the corresponding military rank.

Royal Warrant, dated 30th April, 1837, Art. 2.

FAMILY PENSIONS.

43. The claims to pension of widows and families of officers are treated under the provisions of such Royal Warrant regulating the grant of pensions to the widows and families of British officers as may be in force at the time being. (See page 243.)

44. The widows and families of officers are also entitled to pensions under the Indian Service Family Pension Regulations, for the benefits of which all officers must, as a condition of their appointment, subscribe from the date of their arrival in India. (See pages 274-280.)

Family Pension Regulations.

LEAVE RULES FOR HONORARY COMMISSIONED OFFICERS, SENIOR ASSISTANT-SURGEONS AND WARRANT OFFICERS OF THE INDIAN ARMY.

(Published in India on 8th November, 1889.)

1. These rules shall apply to—

(a) All combatants and medical warrant officers of the Indian Army promoted to warrant rank on or after the date of their publication.

(b) Honorary commissioned officers, senior assistant-surgeons, and warrant officers who may elect them within one year of the date of their publication.

2. Those who apply for leave of any kind within one year of the date of publication of these rules must make their election when submitting their applications for leave; all others, whether on duty or on furlough or leave on the date of publication of these rules, may elect at convenience within the specified time.

3. All honorary commissioned officers, senior assistant-surgeons, and warrant officers who elect these rules will come under their provisions from the date of the receipt of their applications at Army Headquarters, or by the Governments of India, Madras, or Bombay, or at the India Office if at home; and they will be entitled to count all past furlough and leave as service for pension under Rule V.

4. Those who elect these rules while on furlough or leave under older regulations will be allowed the option of continuing to draw any furlough or leave allowances they may be in receipt of under those regulations, if more advantageous, until the expiry of such furlough or leave or any extension thereof, subject to the limit of two years' absence from duty.

5. It must be clearly understood that honorary commissioned officers, senior assistant-surgeons,

or warrant officers who do not elect these rules within the period stated will be held to have declined them, and will be allowed no further opportunity of electing them.

RULES.

I. An honorary commissioned officer, senior assistant-surgeon, or warrant officer subject to these rules may be granted—

(a) Privilege leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.

(b) Leave in or out of India for one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty under conditions stated below.

II. During any time within one year spent on leave in India other than privilege leave an honorary commissioned officer or combatant warrant officer will draw the warrant pay and allowances of his rank and the half staff salary of his appointment, or, if the pay of his appointment be a consolidated salary, then the warrant pay and allowances of his rank and half the difference between such pay and allowances and his consolidated salary; and within the same period a member of the Subordinate Medical Department will draw three-fourths of his grade pay and of his house rent. After one year, and up to the limit of two years, an honorary commissioned officer or combatant warrant officer will draw half of the warrant pay and allowances of his rank and of the staff salary of his appointment, or half of his consolidated salary, as the case may be; and a member of the Subordinate Medical Department will draw half of his grade pay and of his house rent.

During leave out of India the following rates of pay will be drawn :—

Per annum.
Deputy Commissary ... .. £ 210
Assistant ... .. 150
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... .. 110
Conductor ... .. 78
Sub-Conductor ... .. 72
Senior Assistant-Surgeon, 1st Grade 170
" " 2nd " " 120
Assistant-Surgeon, 1st Grade ... .. 85
Assistant " 2nd " " 65
Assistant Apothecary, 1st Grade ... .. 50
Assistant " 2nd " " 40
Sub-Assistant Apothecary ... .. 30

III. Leave may be granted at any time at the discretion of the Commanders-in-Chief of the several Armies, the Governor-General in Council, or the Governors in Council, as the case may be.

IV. Leave in or out of India in excess of one year cannot be claimed as a right, but extensions for any period up to two years' absence from duty in all may be granted on medical certificate or on special or urgent grounds, at the discretion of the sanctioning authority. The previous assent of the authority under whom the individual may be serving will be required for such extensions, except on medical certificate, and must be obtained by the applicant. Extensions beyond two years' absence from duty will only be admissible in very special cases, and without any pay whatever.

V. Leave in or out of India will reckon as service for pension, and will not involve forfeiture of appointment.

VI. Leave will commence on the date of being struck off duty, except that in the case of an officer having to leave his own station to appear before a Medical Board at another station, the date of being struck off duty will mean the date on which he quits the station where he is examined and invalidated. In cases of leave out of India on medical certificate the period of leave recommended by a Medical Board as necessary will be held to commence on the date of quitting

India; the interval between being struck off duty and quitting India will be leave in India. In all cases, however, an officer will be due to rejoin his appointment at the expiry of the period granted to him, the day on which he rejoins it being reckoned as a day of duty.

VII. An honorary commissioned officer, senior assistant-surgeon, or warrant officer unable on account of ill health to return to his duty within the maximum period of two years' absence will, unless specially granted leave without pay, be placed on temporary half pay, or on the retired list, according to the circumstances of his case. An undue amount of leave on account of ill health will likewise render an officer liable to be transferred to half-pay or to the retired list, and the power of such transfer will be exercised by the Government of the Presidency or the Secretary of State for India, as the case may be.

VIII. An honorary commissioned officer, senior assistant-surgeon, or warrant officer on leave in or out of India will be required to rejoin his appointment at once on being recalled to duty on account of the exigencies of the service, unless pronounced by a Medical Board to be unfit to do so. He will be granted a free passage or not, according to the transport regulations in force at the time.

IX. When it may be necessary to convert sterling rates of pay under these rules into rupee currency, or vice versa, the conversion will be made at the rate of exchange in force for the period when the pay becomes due, as fixed annually for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Indian and Imperial Governments.

X. The rules which govern the allowances of honorary commissioned officers, senior assistant-surgeons, and warrant officers acting for absentees remain unchanged.

XI. Advances of pay on proceeding on, or returning from, leave will be granted in accordance with the rules laid down in Army Regulations, India, Vol. I., Part 1.

XII. The Secretary of State for India in Council reserves the right of making any change in the foregoing rules which circumstances may require.

CONDITIONS OF TRANSFER OF HONORARY COMMISSIONED AND WARRANT OFFICERS TO TEMPORARY HALF-PAY.

(Sanctioned by Despatch to Government of India, No. 25, dated 24th January, 1884.)

An honorary commissioned or warrant officer transferred to temporary half-pay may be permitted to remain on half-pay till his total continuous absence from duty, on furlough and half-pay together, amounts to four years. If then still unfit to return to duty, he must be pensioned on the rate of pension for which he had qualified at the time of being placed on half-pay.

No period spent on half-pay will count as service for Indian pension, nor will an honorary commissioned or warrant officer be eligible for promotion while he is on half-pay. An honorary commissioned or warrant officer replaced on the effective list, on return from

half-pay, will be re-employed at once, being borne as a supernumerary in his grade until absorbed by the next vacancy.

Honorary commissioned and warrant officers will be required to appear before a Medical Board at the expiration of one year on half-pay, and again, if retained on the half-pay list, at the end of the second year; and those who, in view to being restored to the effective list, may wish to appear before the Board at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Honorary commissioned or warrant officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will be allowed, in this country, their bonâ fide travelling expenses, and lodging allowance at

the rates stated below, but those appearing at other times, and at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

Rates of Lodging Allowance.

Deputy Commissary ... } 2s. 3d. per diem.
Senior Assistant Surgeon
after three years' service in the first grade ...
Assistant Commissary ... } 2s. per diem.
Deputy Assistant Commissary ...
Senior Assistant Surgeon, first grade, under three years ...
Senior Assistant Surgeon, second grade ... } 1s. 4d. per diem.
Conductor ...
Sub-Conductor ...
Other Medical Warrant Officers

PENSION REGULATIONS FOR HONORARY COMMISSIONED AND WARRANT OFFICERS.

(These Rules took effect from the 1st September, 1881.)

1. All Honorary Commissioned or Warrant Officers on the Establishment on 16th September, 1881, are allowed the option of electing the old or the new scale of pensions at any time previous to retirement. The distinction between the invalid pension, which can be drawn only in India, and the retiring pension is abolished.

2. The periods of service entitling Honorary Commissioned or Warrant Officers to retiring pensions are as follows:—

  • 24 years' total service in Europe and India, including 16 years in the department.
  • 26 years' total service in Europe and India, including 14 years in the department.
  • 28 years' total service in Europe and India, including 12 years in the department.
  • 3. The retiring pensions are—
England or the Colonies. India.
Per annum. Per mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £216 ... Rs. 200
Assistant Commissary 144 ... 140
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 108 ... 100
Conductor ... 90 ... 85
Sub-Conductor ... 72 ... 70

4. An Honorary Commissioned or Warrant Officer of any grade, wishing to retire after 21 years' total service, receives a pension of £54 per annum in England or the Colonies, or Rs. 50 per mensem in India.

5. An Honorary Commissioned or Warrant Officer declared by a Medical Board to be unfit for further service in India, after completion of half the periods of service, army and departmental, laid down in paragraph 2, receives—

England or the Colonies. India.
Per annum. Per mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £172 ... Rs. 160
Assistant Commissary 115 ... 110
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 86 ... 80
Conductor ... 72 ... 68
Sub-Conductor ... 58 ... 56

6. If he has served less than half the prescribed periods, but more than 14 years altogether, he receives—

England or the Colonies. India.
Per annum. Per mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £115 ... Rs. 110
Assistant Commissary 86 ... 80
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 72 ... 68
Conductor ... 58 ... 56
Sub-Conductor ... 43 ... 40

7. If he has not served 14 years altogether, his case will be specially considered.

8. An Honorary Commissioned or Warrant Officer, whose disqualification for further service in India has been caused by intemperance or irregular habits, receives, if he has served half the periods of service, army and departmental, laid down in paragraph 2—

England or the Colonies. India.
Per annum. Per mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £130 ... Rs. 120
Assistant Commissary 86 ... 85
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 65 ... 60
Conductor ... 54 ... 50
Sub-Conductor ... 43 ... 42

ROYAL INDIAN MARINE.

PARTICULARS RELATING TO FIRST APPOINTMENTS.

All first appointments of Officers and Engineers in the Royal Indian Marine are made by the Secretary of State for India in Council.

The limits of age for appointment to the junior executive rank and that of Sub-Lieutenant, are 17 and 22 years, and no candidate will be appointed who does not possess a Board of Trade certificate as Second Mate.

An application for appointment must be made on the prescribed form, which can be procured from the India Office. It must be accompanied by the candidate's Board of Trade certificate, and by certificates of his birth and character, which will be returned after being noted. It will be recorded, but will not be submitted for the orders of the Secretary of State until there is a vacancy.

The selection of candidates is not affected by their absence from England or otherwise.

A candidate must pass an examination by the Medical Board prior to appointment.

On appointment a first-class passage to Bombay will be provided, and an advance of pay, not exceeding £25, recoverable in India, will be made in aid of outfit.

Pay commences from date of embarkation for India.

Service for pension reckons from date of arrival in India.

As a condition of their appointment to the Royal Indian Marine, Officers will be required to subscribe, from date of arrival in India, under the Indian Military Service Family Pension Regulations for the benefits of family pensions.

The names of candidates will be removed from the list on their attaining the age of 22 years.

Any further information may be obtained on application to the Military Secretary, India Office.

For conditions of appointment of Assistant Engineer, see page 292.

EXTRACTS FROM MARINE CIRCULAR BY THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA, NO. 26, DATED 11TH OCTOBER 1883 (AS SUBSEQUENTLY AMENDED).

The following establishment of officers for the Royal Indian Marine, and rates of pay and allowances, have been sanctioned by Her Majesty's Government:—

25 Commanders ... { On pay ranging from Rs. 350 to Rs. 500 per mensem, in addition to staff or command pay.

Lieutenants under three years' seniority ... { Lieutenants under three years' seniority ...
On Rs. 150 per mensem.

13 Sub-Lieutenants { On Rs. 100 per mensem.

This establishment is subject to such increase or decrease in any grades as may be required by any addition to, or reduction in, the vessels of the Royal Indian Marine.

2. The following command and charge allowances are also sanctioned.

MARINE CIRCULAR No. 12 DATED 5TH OCTOBER, 1891.

Pay of Engineers and Assistant Engineers of the Royal Indian Marine.

With the sanction of Her Majesty's Secretary of State for India, the Governor-General in Council is pleased to notify the following regulations regarding the pay and position of the Engineers

and Assistant Engineers of the Royal Indian Marine, in supersession of Marine Circular No. 13 of 2nd November, 1887.

2. The following establishment of Engineers

for the Royal Indian Marine serving afloat, and rates of pay and allowances, have been sanctioned by Her Majesty's Government :—

No. Grades. Rates of pay per mensem.
6 Chief Engineers... 3 on Rs. 400.
49 Engineers ... .. 3 on Rs. 300.
On Rs. 175, rising to Rs. 250, according length of approved service.
Assistant Engineers On Rs. 125 and Rs. 150
9 Engine Drivers 1st class On Rs. 75, rising by annual increments of Rs. 5 to Rs. 100 a month.

3. In addition to these rates of pay, Chief Engineers, Engineers, and Assistant Engineers, when in charge of engines of ships in commission, will be allowed—

When in charge of engines of— Rs. A.
500 and under 750 I.H.P.... 1 8 per diem.
750 " 1,000 " ... 1 12 "
1,000 " 1,500 " ... 2 0 "
1,500 " 2,000 " ... 2 8 "
2,000 " 2,500 " ... 3 3 "
2,500 and over ... .. 5 0 "
When in charge of engines under 500 I.H.P. or of ships in reserve or out of commission, or of the yard craft establishment... .. 1 0 "

4. The above-mentioned allowance will in each case be drawn by the Engineer permanently appointed to the charge of the engines, and it will be continued to him during privilege leave, or when detached by competent authority on any temporary duty not exceeding twenty-eight days when no deputation or other allowances are drawn, and also during absence off duty in sick quarters for any period not exceeding forty-two days. In such cases no allowance is admissible to the Engineer appointed to the temporary charge of the engines.

5. Officers of the Engineer Establishment drawing charge allowance under the foregoing rules will also draw rations, or compensation in lieu, under existing rules.

6. The Director of the Royal Indian Marine is authorised to detail officers of the Engineer Establishment of the Indian Marine for temporary service for other duties whenever he considers it necessary to do so.

7. There shall be no limit to the number in the grade of Engineer so long as the sanctioned establishment for the two grades of Engineer and Assistant Engineer is not exceeded.

8. Assistant Engineers joining without certificates will be entered on Rs. 125 per mensem, and on obtaining certificates of competency as 2nd-class Engineers, either from the Board of Trade, or from a Colony, or from one of the Local Government Boards in India, will be advanced to Rs. 150 per mensem. They will remain on this scale for three years after obtaining certifi-

cates of competency as 1st-class Engineers before they will be entitled to promotion to the grade of Engineer.

9. Assistant Engineers joining with certificates of competency as 2nd-class Engineers, either from the Board of Trade, or from a Colony, or from one of the Local Government Boards in India, will be entered on Rs. 150 per mensem. They will remain on this scale for three years after obtaining certificates of competency as 1st-class Engineers before they will be entitled to promotion to the grade of Engineer.

10. Assistant Engineers joining with a 1st-class certificate of competency, either from the Board of Trade, or from a Colony, or from one of the Local Government Boards in India, will be entered on Rs. 150 per mensem, and be entitled to promotion to the grade of Engineer after three years' service.

11. Engineers will be entitled to the following rates of pay :—

After 3 years' approved service as Engineers, Rs. 200 per mensem.

After 5 years' approved service as Engineers, Rs. 225 per mensem.

After 7 years' approved service as Engineers, Rs. 250 per mensem.

QUALIFICATIONS REQUIRED FOR APPOINTMENT AND PROMOTION.

For Assistant Engineer.

12. The candidate must not be less than 18 nor more than 25 years of age, and he must produce certificates of service in a steam factory for at least four years. If not in possession of a certificate of competency as a 2nd-class or 1st-class Engineer, he will, if in India, be examined by the Inspector of Machinery of the dockyard and a Chief or other senior Engineer, and such other person as the Director or Deputy Director of the Royal Indian Marine may think fit to select, as to his ability as a workman at the several trades, a knowledge of which is requisite for the performance of his duties; he will also be examined in the practical use of the various instruments employed in the engine-room, and in the duties generally of a Marine Engineer; and no candidate will be entered unless the examining Officers certify that his skill as a workman, his knowledge of machinery, and his good and steady conduct fully qualify him for the appointment.

Engineer.

13. To qualify for appointment as an Engineer, an Officer must have served as an Assistant Engineer on Rs. 150 per mensem in an Indian Government vessel for three years, and be in possession of a certificate of competency as a 1st-class Engineer either from the Board of Trade, or from a Colony, or from one of the Local Government Boards in India.

14. The Government of India reserve the right which they have always exercised of admitting Officers from other services or departments to any class of the establishment or of making promotions on public grounds irrespective of seniority.

15. Promotions to the grade of Chief Engineer will be made wholly by selection, by the Government of India, on the recommendation of the Director of the Royal Indian Marine. Chief Engineers will be promoted to the Rs. 400 grade only after twenty years' service.

16. When certificated and uncertificated Engineers are serving on board the same vessel, the certificated Engineers will take precedence above the uncertificated Engineers.

17. A ship's Engineer transferred to a dock-yard appointment will be seconded, and a promotion made in his place. If brought back again on the establishment, he will be borne as supernumerary till absorbed. Officers temporarily transferred will not be seconded; but

acting promotions will be made in such cases, and in the room of Officers absent on furlough or leave.

NOTE.—In paragraphs 8, 9, 10, and 13, the certificates of competency referred to as being obtained from one of the Local Government Boards in India are those equivalent to the Board of Trade or Colonial Certificates, and do not include those known as "First-class Local Indian Home Trade Certificates."

FURLOUGH AND LEAVE RULES OF THE ROYAL INDIAN MARINE.
MARINE CIRCULAR OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA, No. 16 OF 1884.

Furlough.

I.—Officers after five years' service in India or under the Government of India may receive one year's furlough, and an additional year for each subsequent five years' service. Furlough not taken at the time it becomes due is not lost, and can, subject to these Regulations, be taken later.

II.—An officer may not be absent from duty on any grounds for more than three years consecutively, nor on return from furlough may he receive, except under Rule VII., any further furlough until after an interval of three years.

III.—*Furlough may be spent anywhere, and when it has been earned by length of service, counts as service for pension. Furlough taken before it is earned by length of service reckons against service for pension till paid off.

IV.—An officer on furlough receives half the pay of his rank, or if holding a shore appointment, half the pay of his appointment, excluding command, charge, acting, personal, or house allowance, computed according to a rate of exchange to be fixed in each year by the Secretary of State. An officer promoted while on furlough will be entitled to a moiety of the increased pay due to that promotion.

V.—†An officer proceeding on or returning from furlough may be granted an advance of three months' furlough pay; and he may further‡ be granted the advance in aid of passage money specified in clause IX. if he does not obtain passage by troopship. Advances made in England will be recovered in India by monthly installments of one-fourth of the pay drawn by the officers. Advances made in India will be noted on the last pay certificates of officers, and no issue of furlough pay will be made till after the sum advanced has accrued as furlough pay.

VI.—Furlough pay may not be drawn for more than two years consecutively.

VII.—Upon certificate of a Medical Board, an officer not entitled to furlough, who has served not less than one year in India, may be granted furlough. Such furlough will not count of service for pension until it has been paid off by subsequent service under the terms of Rule I. Furlough cannot be anticipated beyond two years in

the aggregate, nor for a second term, unless that previously anticipated has been subsequently paid off under the terms of Rule I.

VIII.—An officer who has exhausted the furlough obtainable under these Rules, and who may be certified by a Medical Board to be still unfit for duty, will be retired on the pension or gratuity to which he may be entitled; if not entitled to either pension or gratuity, his case will be specially considered (but see under heading "Temporary Retirement," on p. lxxxvi.).

IX.—§Marine officers not holding shore appointments of Rs. 1,000 per mensem will be eligible for the grant of non-regulation passages in troopships when such passages are available. Officers who do not obtain passages in troopships will be allowed advances† in aid of passage money, in addition to the advances admissible under Clause V., provided that no officer shall in any case draw advances exceeding three months' pay in all.

X.—Furlough, whether on private affairs or on medical certificate, is restricted to a maximum period of one year in the first instance, and to subsequent extensions not exceeding six months at a time.

General Leave.

XI.—General leave may be granted for any period not exceeding six months at a time, but cannot be renewed, except for very urgent reasons, till after two years have elapsed from its expiration. It may be spent anywhere, does not involve forfeiture of appointment, and counts as service for pension. It will be granted or refused entirely at the discretion of the Director of the Indian Marine. It may be granted up to a maximum limit of 30 days as subsidiary to furlough, whether on proceeding on furlough or on return from furlough. Leave granted as subsidiary to furlough can only be spent in India.

XII.—During general leave an officer will be restricted to the pay of his grade,|| and will forfeit all allowances, which will be paid to his locum tenens. If an officer exceeds the six months' leave admissible at one time under this Rule, the entire period will be converted into furlough, and his allowances adjusted accordingly.

* As modified by Marine Circular, No. 6 of 1885.

† As modified by Marine Circular, No. 7 of 1886.

‡ Unless holding shore appointments of Rs. 1,000 per mensem. Such officers are ineligible for passage by troopship (cl. ix.) and are also ineligible for any advance exceeding three months' Furlough pay (Marine Circular, No. 1 of 1888).

§ As modified by Marine Circular, No. 31 of 1885.

|| This Rule will also hold good in the case of an officer holding a shore appointment, in which case the balance will be considered a staff allowance payable to the locum tenens provided the latter does not thereby draw more than the pay of the appointment.

Privilege Leave.

XIII.—Subject to the exigencies of the service, Marine officers may be allowed 60 days' privilege leave during each year, and each leave may be taken in instalments.

XIV.—An officer who has privilege leave to his credit when proceeding on leave or furlough does not forfeit it, but privilege leave cannot be taken in continuation of furlough, nor can furlough be obtained in continuation of privilege leave. Privilege leave may, however, be converted into general leave with the sanction of the Director of the Indian Marine, or into furlough with the sanction of the Government of India.

XV.—Privilege leave will be granted by the Director of the Indian Marine, but it will be granted only on the understanding that no additional expense is thereby caused to the State, either directly or indirectly, that the applicant's services can be spared, and that satisfactory arrangements for the performance of his duties during his absence can be made.

XVI.—Officers are at liberty to proceed wherever they may desire when on privilege leave, provided they can ensure their return before the expiration of the leave. If an officer exceeds his privilege leave, the whole period of his absence will be converted into general leave or furlough, and his allowances and statement of service for furlough adjusted accordingly.

Temporary Leave.

XVII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 10 days, and not in anticipation or extension of any kind of leave or furlough, may be granted by the Director and Deputy Director of the Indian Marine.

XVIII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 72 hours may be granted by commanders of vessels and heads of departments to officers under their command.

General Regulations.

XIX.—It must be distinctly understood that furlough or leave cannot be claimed as a right. Both are subject to the exigencies of the public service, and will be granted only when an officer's services can be spared. Commanders of vessels and heads of departments will not forward an application for furlough or leave unless they are able to satisfy the Director of the Indian Marine that the officer's services can be spared without any inconvenience to the public service.

XX.—No grant of leave given under the conditions of these Rules will count against an officer's service for pension, and leave may be spent anywhere, i.e., within possible limits of return before it expires.

XXI.—Leave is reckoned from the day on which an officer avails himself of it, and it expires on the day he rejoins.

Furlough reckons from date of leaving India, or from date of leaving his ship if at an out-station, unless specially stated by authority to commence on an earlier date; and in the event

of an officer returning to India before the expiration of his furlough, it ceases on the date of his return.

The day of return, whether from leave or from furlough, is a day of duty.

XXII.—Officers on furlough will draw their pay from the India Office, but officers on general or privilege leave must arrange to draw their pay in India.

XXIII.—As a rule leave will be granted only during the period of the south-west monsoon.

XXIV.—Officers proceeding on furlough will report their departure personally at the office of the Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine, except when they avail themselves of it at an out-station. An officer returning from furlough or leave will rejoin the ship to which he belongs; but if this is impracticable, and he has not received instructions (which he should apply for beforehand), he will rejoin at Bombay or Calcutta, reporting himself in person to the Director or Deputy Director. On the departure of an officer on furlough or leave, or on the return of an officer therefrom, the commander of the vessel will cause an entry to be made of the date in the ship's log-book, and will forward an extract thereof by the next post to the Director.

XXV.—Officers arriving in Europe on furlough or on general leave* must report their arrival in writing to the Military Secretary at the India Office, and state their address; and for extension of furlough or for permission to return to duty, they must also apply to the Military Secretary. †Before applying for extension of furlough on private affairs they must obtain permission to do so from the Government of India, through the Director of Marine. The permission thus obtained must be attached to the application for extension, and submitted to the Military Secretary at the India Office.

Officers proceeding to the colonies on furlough should apply to India for extension of furlough and for permission to return to duty.

XXVI.—During the absence of an officer on leave or furlough, he must keep the Director of the Indian Marine acquainted with his actual address, and be prepared to return immediately to India, should his services be required. When on leave, such recall order will be issued by the Director; when on furlough in Europe, by the Secretary of State. Officers spending their furlough in Europe must keep the Secretary of State informed of their address.

XXVII.—Applications for leave or furlough must be made on the authorised form, which is obtainable from the Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine.

NOTE.—(Marine Circular No. 10 of 1885).

The above rules are applicable to—

  1. I.—Officers on the Graded and Super-numerary List of Executive Officers.
  2. II.—Officers on the Graded List of Engineers.
  3. III.—Gazetted clerks,
  4. IV.—Warrant officers who may be specially admitted to the rules.
MARINE CIRCULAR No. 7. DATED 26TH APRIL, 1892.

With reference to Marine Circular No. 16 of the 1st November, 1884, the Governor-General in Council is pleased, under instructions from Her

Majesty's Secretary of State for India, to publish the following revised rules regarding the grant of leave to officers of the Royal Indian Marine.

* Marine Circular No. 19 of 1888.† Marine Circular No. 3 of 1889.

2. These rules shall apply to—

  • (a) All officers appointed to the Indian Marine after the date of their publication.
  • (b) Officers who may elect them within one year of the date of their publication.

NOTE.—The term “officers” includes the Graded and Supernumerary List of Executive Officers and the Graded List of Engineers.

3. Those who apply for leave of any kind within one year of the date of publication of these rules must make their election when submitting their applications for leave; all others, whether on duty or on furlough or leave on the date of publication of these rules, may elect at convenience within the specified time.

4. All officers who elect these rules will come under their provisions from the date of the receipt of their applications by the Government of India if they are in India, or at the India Office if they are at home; and they will be entitled to count all past furlough and leave as service for pension under Rule V.

5. Those who elect these rules while on furlough or general leave under the previous regulations will be allowed the option of continuing to draw any furlough or leave allowances they may be in receipt of under those regulations, if more advantageous, until the expiry of such furlough or general leave or any extension thereof which would have been admissible under the previous regulations, subject to the limit of two years' absence from duty.

6. It must be clearly understood that officers who do not elect these rules within the period stated will be held to have declined them, and will be allowed no further opportunity of electing them.

7. “General leave” and “subsidiary leave” will not be granted to officers under these rules.

Rules.

I.—An officer subject to these rules may be granted—

  • (a) Privilege leave and temporary leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.
  • (b) Leave in or out of India for one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty under conditions stated below.

Leave in or out of India other than privilege or temporary leave.

II.—During leave in or out of India other than privilege or temporary leave, the following rates of pay will be drawn, whether an officer holds a shore appointment or not:—

Rs. per mensem.
Commanders, 1st Grade... 410
    "    2nd Grade 330
    "    3rd Grade 280
1st-Grade officers of six years' seniority 210
    "    of three " 170
    "    under ditto 130
2nd-Grade officers 120
3rd-Grade officers 110
Chief Engineers on Rs. 400 per mensem 320
    "    on Rs. 300 per mensem 270
Engineers" on Rs. 250 230
    "    on Rs. 225 215
    "    on Rs. 200 200
    "    on Rs. 175 190
Assistant Engineers on Rs. 150 165
    "    on Rs. 125 150

An officer promoted while on leave will be entitled to the higher rate of leave pay from date of promotion.

III.—Leave may be granted at any time at the discretion of the Government of India, on the recommendation of the Director of the Royal Indian Marine. (See addendum in No. 13. of 1802.)

IV.—Extensions of leave in or out of India in excess of one year, but not exceeding two years' absence from duty in all, may be granted on medical certificate or on special or urgent grounds, at the discretion of the sanctioning authority. The previous assent of the Government of India will be required for extensions, except on medical certificate, and must be obtained by the applicant through the Director of the Royal Indian Marine. Extensions beyond two years' absence from duty will only be admissible in very special cases, and without any pay whatever.

V.—Leave in or out of India will reckon as service for pension.

VI.—Leave will commence on the date of being struck off duty, except that in the case of an officer having to appear before a Medical Board the date of being struck off duty will mean the date on which he quits the station where he is examined and invalidated. In cases of leave out of India on medical certificate the period of leave recommended by a Medical Board as necessary will be held to commence on the date of quitting India. In all cases, however, an officer will be due to rejoin his ship or appointment at the expiry of the period granted to him, the day on which he rejoins it being reckoned as a day of duty.

VII.—An officer unable on account of ill health to return to his duty within the maximum period of two years' absence will, unless specially granted leave without pay, be placed on temporary or permanent retirement, according to the circumstances of his case. An undue amount of leave on account of ill health will likewise render an officer liable to be transferred to temporary or permanent retirement.

VIII.—An officer on leave in or out of India will be required to rejoin his appointment at once on being recalled to duty on account of the exigencies of the service, unless pronounced by a Medical Board to be unfit to do so.
